claims.claim: The SEI layer can decompose exothermically at 90–120 °C.
claims.quote: This layer is metastable and can decompose exothermically at 90–120 8C.

claims.claim: At elevated temperatures above 120 °C, the SEI layer does not protect the negative electrode from contact with the electrolyte, and an exothermic reaction between intercalated lithium and electrolyte can occur.
claims.quote: At elevated temperatures (>120 8C), the SEI layer does not protect the negative electrode from contact with the electrolyte and an exothermic reaction between inter- calated lithium and electrolyte can occur.

claims.claim: Maleki et al. reported that the reaction of LixC6 with PVDF begins at about 200 °C and reaches a maximum at 287 °C.
claims.quote: Maleki et al. [3] report a value of 317 J/g for the reaction of LixC6 with PVDF; the reaction begins at (cid:3)200 8C and reaches a maximum at 287 8C.

claims.claim: In ARC simulations, SEI decomposition begins first at 70 °C.
claims.quote: The ARC simulations (Fig. 2) indicate the SEI decom- position will begin ﬁrst at 70 8C.

claims.claim: The simulations indicate that the negative electrode binder plays a relatively unimportant role in thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The simulations indicate that the negative electrode bin- der plays a relatively unimportant role in thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Under normal battery operating conditions, electrolyte tends to be oxidized/reduced on the surface of a highly delithiated cathode or fully lithiated anode, leading to gas production (especially in the formation cycles).
claims.quote:
Under  normal  battery  operating
conditions,  electrolyte  tends  to  be  oxidized/educed  on  the
surface  of  the  highly  delithiated  cathode/fully  lithiated  anode,
leading  to  gas  production,  especially  in  the  formation  cycles.

claims.claim: Overheating is a trigger for thermal runaway, which occurs from overcharging, overheating, external short circuits, or internal short circuits, resulting in a fast increase of battery internal temperature.
claims.quote:
Overheating is a trigger for the thermal runaway, which occurs
from  overcharging,[8b,10]  overheating,[4a,11]  external  short  cir-
cuits[12] or internal short circuits,[13] resulting in fast increase of
battery internal temperature.[1a]

claims.claim: When the temperature reaches a certain level, the formed SEI layer and separator start to decompose and melt, producing more heat and gases.
claims.quote:
When the temperature reaches
a certain level, the formed solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer
and  separator  start  to  decompose  and  melt,  producing  more
heat  and  gases.[1a,14]

claims.claim: If the internal temperature exceeds the thermal stability temperature of cathodes (e.g., NMC cathodes), oxygen release will occur to provide more combustion-supporting agent for subsequent combustion explosion of the battery.
claims.quote:
Eventually,  if  the  internal  temperature
exceeds  the  thermal  stability  temperature  of  cathodes,  like
NMC  cathodes,[4a,15]  oxygen  release  will  occur  to  provide  more
combustion-supporting  agent  for  the  subsequent  combustion
explosion  of  battery.

claims.claim: Nika et al. found Li2CO3 tends to decompose into CO2 at a relative low voltage of 3.8 V, based on the reaction 2Li2CO3→4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + 1O2.
claims.quote:
found that it tends to decompose into CO2 based on the decom-
position reaction of 2Li2CO3→4Li+ + 4e− + 2CO2 + 1O2 at a rela-
tive  low  voltage  of  3.8  V

claims.claim: In nail penetration, Joule heat is mainly concentrated at the contact surface between the nail and the battery.
claims.quote: The Joule heat generated in nail penetration process is mainly concentrated in the contact surface between the nail and the battery.

claims.claim: In the nail penetration scenario, contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so total resistance is dominated by contact resistance.
claims.quote: In the nail penetration  scenario,  the  contact  resistance  is  usually  several  orders  of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so the total resistance is dominated by the contact resistance

claims.claim: Chen et al. reported that, under the inhomogeneous contact resistance assumption, the maximum heat production was always 300% higher than under the uniformity assumption.
claims.quote: the  maximum  heat production  under  the  inhomogeneous  assumption  was  always  300  % higher  than  that  under  the  uniformity  assumption.

claims.claim: LiPF6 salt decomposes at 107 °C and generates HF via reactions LiPF6 → LiF + PF5, PF5 + H2O → POF3 + 2HF, and LiPF6 + H2O → LiF + POF3 + 2HF.
claims.quote:
LiPF6 salt decomposes at 107 

◦

C and generates HF:

LiPF6 → LiF + PF5

(42) 

LiPF6 + H2O → LiF + POF3 + 2HF

(43)  

PF5 + H2O → POF3 + 2HF

claims.claim: Different safety valve structures have different venting pressures ranging from 1 MPa to 3.448 MPa.
claims.quote:
Different
safety valve structures have different venting pressures, ranging from 1
MPa to 3.448Mpa

claims.claim: When battery temperature rises above 90 °C, exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decompositions occur and thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises to above 90 (cid:3)C, a series of exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decom-positions takes place, and thermal runaway occurs.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests on coin cells, adding about 4 wt.% pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature by about 60%.
claims.quote: In nail penetration test on coin cells, (cid:2)4 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature by (cid:2)60%;

claims.claim: In impact tests on pouch cells, adding about 5 wt.% pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature by about 90%.
claims.quote: in impact test on pouch cells, (cid:2)5 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature by (cid:2)90%.

claims.claim: Pentadecane is immiscible with the electrolyte and is more wettable to the separator; as a result, it repels electrolyte and forms a physical blocking layer in the separator that suppresses Li+ transport.
claims.quote: Since pentadecane is immisci-ble with the electrolyte and is more wettable to the separator, it will repel electrolyte and form a physical blocking layer in the separator, which suppresses Liþ transport.

claims.claim: According to EIS fitted results, the series resistance Rs is 1.72 Ω for the reference cell and 85.1 Ω for the pentadecane-modified cell, and the charge transfer resistance Rct is 375.2 Ω for the reference cell and 20004.3 Ω for the pentadecane-modified cell.
claims.quote: According to the EIS ﬁtted results in Fig. 2(b), the series resistance (Rs) for reference and pentadecane-modiﬁed cells are 1.72 X and 85.1 X, respectively; the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for refer-ence and pentadecane-modiﬁed cells are 375.2 X and 20004.3 X, respectively.

claims.claim: Fire incidents during charging sessions account for approximately 26% of all cases, and are attributed to direct battery ignition, charging equipment malfunctions, and overcharging.
claims.quote: fire incidents during  charging  sessions  account  for  approximately  26%  of  all  cases,  attributed  to  direct  battery ignition,  charging  equipment  malfunctions,  and  overcharging.

claims.claim: Fires occurring while vehicles are parked represent about 13% of incidents, particularly in climates with considerable day-to-night temperature variations, exacerbated by drivers' lack of safety awareness.
claims.quote: fires  occurring  while vehicles are parked represent a significant portion (about 13%) of incidents, particularly in climates with  considerable  day-to-night  temperature  variations,  exacerbated  by  drivers'  lack  of  safety awareness.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in the battery pack, upon reaching a critical threshold, triggers fires and other hazardous events.
claims.quote: thermal runaway within the battery pack, which,  upon  reaching  a  critical  threshold,  triggers  fires  and  other  hazardous  events.

claims.claim: Due to poor thermal stability of lithium-ion batteries, thermal runaway is prone to occur under external abuses such as overcharging, crushing, and collision.
claims.quote: Due  to  the  poor  thermal  stability  of  lithium-ion  batteries,  thermal  runaway  is  prone  to  occur  in external abuses such as overcharging, crushing, and collision.

claims.claim: In thermal runaway, a chain exothermic reaction occurs in the cell monomer, leading to an uncontrollable rise in cell temperature.
claims.quote: A chain exothermic reaction occurs in the cell monomer under thermal runaway, leading to an uncontrollable rise in cell temperature

claims.claim: When the local temperature at the internal shorting site reaches about 110 °C, secondary reactions including decomposition of electrolyte and electrodes are activated.
claims.quote:
When T reaches (cid:2)110 (cid:3)C, a number of secondary
reactions would be activated, including the decomposition of
electrolyte and electrodes.

claims.claim: The triggering temperature for thermally triggered thermal-runaway mitigation mechanisms must be much higher than the LIB operation/storage temperature range, around 110–130 °C, which is already at the critical point of thermal runaway (about 120 °C).
claims.quote:
The triggering temperature must be much
higher than the operation/storage temperature range of LIB,
around 110–130 (cid:3)C, which is already at the critical point of
thermal runaway ((cid:2)120 (cid:3)C).

claims.claim: In the reference cell impact test, the peak temperature increase (ΔTref) is about 5 °C and is reached in about half a minute.
claims.quote:
The
peak temperature increase of the reference cell, DTref, is
(cid:2)5 (cid:3)C, which is reached in about half a minute

claims.claim: With a heterogeneous current collector, the temperature increase (ΔT) during impact is around 30% of ΔTref, suggesting that heat generation associated with internal shorting is negligible.
claims.quote:
The increase in the resulting temperature,
DT, is around 30% of DTref, close to that of a fully dis-
charged half-cell, suggesting that the heat generation associ-
ated with internal shorting is negligible.

claims.claim: Cycling tests were conducted at 1C rate from 3 V to 4.3 V, and reference and modified cells showed nearly the same behavior.
claims.quote:
The cycling experiment was conducted at
1C rate from 3 V to 4.3 V... The behaviors of reference and modiﬁed cells are nearly
the same

claims.claim: Thermal runaway (TR) is a self-sustaining, uncontrollable temperature increase within a battery that leads to cascading chemical reactions and releases large amounts of heat and gas, potentially causing fires or explosions within a few minutes.
claims.quote: TR refers to a self-sustaining, uncontrollable increase in temperature within the battery, leading to a cascade of chemical reactions and the release of large amounts of heat and gas. This can ultimately result in fires, explosions, and other hazardous situations within few minutes.

claims.claim: T1 is the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to solid electrolyte interface (SEI) decomposition.
claims.quote: T1 signifies the temperature at which detectable self-heating of the battery begins due to solid elec- trolyte interface (SEI) decomposition.

claims.claim: T2 is the temperature at which TR is triggered, often associated with separator failure leading to an internal short circuit (ISC), and at T2 the temperature transitions from a stable level to a quasi-exponential increase.
claims.quote: T2 represents the temperature at which TR is triggered, often associated with separator failure leading to an internal short circuit (ISC). At T2, the temperature transitions from a stable level to a quasi-exponential increase

claims.claim: The onset of self-heating temperature T1 is reached when the battery’s temperature rise rate surpasses an empirically determined threshold.
claims.quote: The onset of self-heating, T1, is reached when the battery’s temperature rise rate surpasses an empirically determined threshold.

claims.claim: Mechanical abuse (nail penetrations, crushing, or dropping) can result in separator tearing; electrical abuse (overcharging, over discharging, or external short circuits) can cause the separator to be pierced; and thermal abuse (overheating, fire, or high ambient temperature) can lead to separator collapse; regardless of how the separator is damaged, an ISC is triggered, causing excessive heat and intensifying electrochemical side reactions.
claims.quote: Mechanical abuse, such as nail penetrations, crushing, or dropping the battery, can result in separator tearing. Similarly, electrical abuse from overcharging, over discharging, or external short circuits (ESC) can cause the separator to be pierced. Thermal abuse, caused by over- heating, fire, or high ambient temperature, can lead to separator collapse. Regardless of how the separator is damaged, an ISC is triggered in the lithium-ion battery, causing excessive heat and intensifying electrochemical side reactions

claims.claim: When the cell temperature exceeded 363.15 K, the SEI film decomposed via an exothermic reaction.
claims.quote: When the temperature of the cell exceeded 363.15 K, the SEI film decomposed via an exothermic reaction.

claims.claim: When the cell temperature exceeded 393.15 K, an exothermic reaction was initiated between the negative electrode and the electrolyte.
claims.quote: When the temperature of the cell exceeded 393.15 K, an exothermic reaction was initiated between the negative electrode and the electrolyte.

claims.claim: When the cell temperature exceeded 443.15 K, an exothermic reaction was initiated between the positive electrode and electrolyte.
claims.quote:
When the temperature of the cell exceeded 443.15 K, an exothermic reaction was initiated between the positive electrode and
electrolyte.

claims.claim: When the cell temperature exceeded 473.15 K, the electrolyte began to decompose.
claims.quote: When the cell temperature exceeded 473.15 K, the electrolyte began to decompose.

claims.claim: The acceptable margin of error for temperature was assumed to be 3 K because a temperature transition zone of 3 K was observed after crossing the critical temperatures.
claims.quote:
The acceptable margin of error for temperature was assumed to be 3 K because the side reactions do not immediately release a large amount
of heat after crossing the critical temperatures; instead, a temperature transition zone of 3 K was observed.

claims.claim: The internal short-circuit (ISC) triggering condition in the model is when the jellyroll node temperature reaches the separator melting point (165 °C for the chosen battery).
claims.quote: The ISC triggering condition is set as when the temperature of the jellyroll node reaches the separator melting point [39, 40] (165 °C for the chosen battery).

claims.claim: In a two-battery experiment, after the first battery runaway reaction is triggered, its temperature increases to approximately 850 °C.
claims.quote:
When the runaway reaction of the ﬁrst battery is triggered, its temperature increases dramatically to ap-
proximately 850 °C.

claims.claim: In the two-battery experiment, the time interval between the two thermal runaway (TR) reactions is approximately 5 seconds.
claims.quote:
The time interval ΔT between the two TR reac-
tions of the cells is approximately 5 sec.

claims.claim: The simulation indicates a threshold equivalent thermal resistance between adjacent batteries of θc = 0.89 K/W that separates two thermal runaway propagation modes.
claims.quote:
The simulation indicates
the threshold
θc = 0.89 K/W.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway propagation speed increases with ambient temperature and increases when battery spacing d decreases.
claims.quote:
First, the TR propagation speed increases with Tamb, because a high
Tamb corresponds to a high initial thermal energy within each battery.
Second, the TR propagation speed increases when battery spacing d
decreases.

claims.claim: The separator porosity–compression strain relationship was fitted as p* = p0 + 0.05118εz − 0.00858εz^2 based on separator compression experiments.
claims.quote:
can be fitted by the function p∗ = p0 + 0.05118εz (cid:0)
0.00858εz
2

claims.claim: When the separator thickness-direction strain exceeds the failure strain (εz > εf), the electrical short circuit is activated.
claims.quote:
Then, once the strain-based failure criterion of the separator (given in
Eq. (2)) is satisfied, the electrical short circuit is activated:

εz > εf

claims.claim: Before reaching the critical failure value, the separator effective resistance varies linearly with thickness-direction strain.
claims.quote:
The  effective  resistance  of  the  separator  has  a  linear  relationship
with the strain component in the thickness direction before reaching the
critical failure value.

claims.claim: In the short-circuit criterion used in the model, the separator resistance is R = R0(1 − εz) for εz < εf, and R = Rsc for εz ≥ εf.
claims.quote:
{

R =

R0(1 (cid:0) εz), εz < εf
Rsc, εz ≥ εf

(3)

claims.claim: The short-circuit current boundary condition is Isc = −∑(n=0 to nst) V(t)/(Rcell + Rsc).
claims.quote:
Thus, the
boundary condition for the short circuit condition can be derived as:

Isc = (cid:0)

∑nst

n=0

V(t)
Rcell + Rsc

claims.claim: Experiments heated 100% SOC pouch LIBs under extrusion to trigger thermal runaway (TR).
claims.quote: Here,  100%  state  of  charge  (SOC)  pouch  LIBs  are heated under extrusion, for triggering TR.

claims.claim: For a squeezed-state battery, thickness gradually decreases as temperature increases, until it drops abruptly at TR onset.
claims.quote: The battery thickness gradually decreases with increasing  the  temperature  of  a squeezed-state battery, until dropping abruptly at the TR onset.

claims.claim: TR onset occurs faster for higher squeezing pressures; jet fire duration is shorter; flame temperature and flame area increase.
claims.quote: The TR onset occurs faster for higher squeezing pressures, the jet fire duration is shorter, while the flame temperature and  area  increase.

claims.claim: After the extrusion force reaches 5000 N, the TR triggering temperature drops to about 100 °C.
claims.quote:
After the extrusion force reaches 5000 N, the TR triggering temperature drops to ~100 
C,

claims.claim: For squeezing pressures above 5000 N, TR triggering is mostly due to extrusion-induced mechanical damage.
claims.quote:
For squeezing pressures above 5000 N, the TR trig-
 gering  temperature  decreased  to  approximately  100 
C,  the  voltage 
 dropped concurrently with the TR onset, the voltage fluctuation before 
 the  TR  disappeared,  and  TR  triggering  was  mostly  owing  to  the 
 extrusion-induced mechanical damage.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway of an NCM811 lithium-ion battery triggered by nail penetration was tested under three cases: full depth@100%SOC, half depth@100%SOC, and full depth@50%SOC.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway (TR) of NCM811 Lithium-ion battery (LIB) triggered by nail penetration was tested under three cases of full depth@100%SOC, half depth@100%SOC, and full depth@50%SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: In full depth@100%SOC versus full depth@50%SOC, the internal peak temperatures were 887.3 °C and 483.2 °C, respectively.
claims.quote: In the case of full depth@100%SOC and full depth@50%SOC, the internal peak temperature is 887.3 °C and 483.2 °C

claims.claim: The results indicate that the effect of SOC on thermal runaway is more significant than that of penetration depth.
claims.quote: The results show that the effect of SOC on thermal runaway is more significant than that of penetration depth.

claims.claim: For different cathode materials, thermal runaway of an NCM811 LIB is more serious than that of an NCM532 LIB.
claims.quote: For different cathode materials, the thermal runaway of NCM811 LIB is more serious than that of NCM532 LIB.

claims.claim: For different trigger modes, thermal runaway of an NCM811 LIB triggered by nail penetration is more serious than that triggered by external overheating.
claims.quote: For different trigger modes, the thermal runaway of NCM811 LIB triggered by nail penetration is more serious than that triggered by external overheating.

claims.claim: The flammability limits (FLs) of battery vent gas (BVG) were measured using a 5 L spherical explosive device.
claims.quote: the flammability limits (FLs) of battery vent gas (BVG) were measured by a 5 L spherical explosive device.

claims.claim: When H2 volume concentration increases from 10% to 30% (at 300 K and 100 kPa), the BVG LFL decreases from 15.65% to 12.3% and the UFL increases from 50.6% to 56.92%.
claims.quote: As the H2  volume concentration increases from 10 % to 30 %, the LFL decreases from 15.65 % to 12.3 %, while the UFL increases from 50.6 % to 56.92 %.

claims.claim: When CO concentration increases from 5% to 50% (at 300 K and 100 kPa), the BVG LFL decreases from 14.8% to 12.71% and the UFL increases from 44.85% to 54.6%.
claims.quote: As the CO concentration increases from 5 % to 50 %, the LFL decreases from 14.8 % to 12.71 %. Meanwhile, the UFL  exhibits  an  opposite  trend,  increasing  from  44.85  %  to  54.6  %.

claims.claim: When CO2 volume concentration increases from 20% to 70% (at 300 K and 100 kPa), the BVG LFL increases from 10.32% to 37.76% and the UFL rises from 51.85% to 64.2%.
claims.quote: As the volume concentration of CO2 increases from 20 % to 70 %, the LFL of the gas mixture increases from 10.32 % to 37.76 %, and the UFL  rises  from  51.85  %  to  64.2  %.

claims.claim: According to ASTM E2079-07, ignition was judged by pressure increase in the explosive device after ignition; in this apparatus, a 7% internal pressure increase was considered ignition.
claims.quote: According to ASTM E2079-07  standard  [20],  whether  the  fuel  is  ignited  is  judged  by  the increase of pressure in the explosive device after ignition... It is found that when the internal pressure of the explosion vessel increases by 7 %, it can be considered that the mixed gas is ignited.

claims.claim: CuO decoration decreases the InSe band gap from 1.938 eV to 0.831 eV.
claims.quote: CuO decoration decreases  the  InSe  band  gap  from  1.938  eV  to  0.831  eV

claims.claim: Compared to pristine InSe, adsorption of CO, C2H2, and C2H4 improves by 130.1%, 76.9%, and 75.7%, respectively, on CuO-InSe.
claims.quote: Adsorption of CO, C₂H₂, and C₂H₄ improves by 130.1 %, 76.9 %, and 75.7 %, respectively, compared to pristine InSe.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, lithium-ion batteries release large amounts of CO, C2H2, C2H4, CO2, H2 and other flammable gases.
claims.quote: During thermal runaway, LIBs release large amounts of CO, C2H2, C2H4, CO2, H2 and other flammable gases.

claims.claim: As thermal runaway continues, gas content can gradually increase, causing serious safety risks.
claims.quote: As thermal runaway continues, gas content can gradually increase, causing serious safety risks.

claims.claim: A negative binding energy value suggests CuO decoration is thermodynamically and structurally stable, and greater negativity indicates higher stability.
claims.quote: a negative value suggests that the decoration is thermodynam-ically and structurally stable, with greater negativity indicating higher stability

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil.
claims.quote: Specifically, thermal runaway is triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak pressure is 430.43 kPa.
claims.quote: the corresponding peak pressure is the highest at 430.43 kPa

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak gas generation is 7.72 mol.
claims.quote:
The heating
coil method shows the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, CO2 gas concentrations were 27.73% (heating coil), 24.6% (heating plate), and 18.08% (heating rod).
claims.quote:
under the same 100 % SOC conditions, the descending order of CO2 gas concentrations is as follows:
heating coil (27.73 %), heating plate (24.6 %), and heating rod (18.08 %).

claims.claim: The flammable concentration ranges were 51.11% (heating rod), 49.82% (heating coil), and 46.94% (heating plate).
claims.quote:
the flammable concentration ranges from
largest to smallest for the three heating methods is: heating rod (51.11 %), heating coil (49.82 %), and heating plate (46.94 %).

claims.claim: In this work, thermal runaway (TR) occurrence was determined by three criteria: Th temperature rise rate exceeding 1 ℃/s, battery mass loss rate continuing to exceed 0.5 g/s, and considerable white smoke ejected from the safety valve.
claims.quote:
In this work, the occurrence of TR was determined as the tempera-
ture rise rate of Th  exceeding 1 ℃/s, the mass loss rate of the battery
continuing to exceed 0.5 g/s and a considerable amount of white smoke
is ejected from the safety valve.

claims.claim: The external heater provided the same heating power density (1.03 W/cm2) to the three different heating positions for comparison.
claims.quote:
The external heater all provided the same (1.03 W/cm2)
heating power density to the three different heating positions of LIBs for

claims.claim: Thermal runaway trigger time (tTR) was 1704 ± 50 s under large surface heating (LSH), 8860 ± 70 s under side heating (SH), and 6244.5 ± 138.5 s under bottom heating (BH).
claims.quote:
As shown in Table 2, the TR trigger time (tTR)
under LSH, SH and BH is 1704 ± 50 s, 8860 ± 70 s and 6244.5 ± 138.5
s, respectively.

claims.claim: Average internal TR propagation velocity (νTR) was 2.035 ± 0.058 mm/s under side heating and 1.942 ± 0.217 mm/s under bottom heating, and these are approximately seven times the value under large surface heating (0.296 ± 0.007 mm/s).
claims.quote:
the values of νTR under SH and BH mode are 2.035 ± 0.058 mm/s
and 1.942 ± 0.217 mm/s, and the value is approximately 7 times greater than that under large surface heating (0.296 ± 0.007 mm/s).

claims.claim: Maximum jet velocity during TR was largest under LSH (13.25 ± 0.255 m/s), followed by SH (11.11 ± 0.78 m/s), and then BH (8.96 ± 0.5505 m/s).
claims.quote:
The maximum  jet  velocity  during  TR  under  LSH  is  the
largest, with a value of 13.25 ± 0.255 m/s, followed SH (11.11 ±
0.78 m/s), and finally the BH (8.96 ± 0.5505 m/s).

claims.claim: During TR propagation in module 1, the maximum temperatures for LIB 1–4 were 401.9 ℃, 437.0 ℃, 510.3 ℃, and 524.7 ℃, respectively, showing an increasing tendency attributed to the pre-heating effect.
claims.quote: The maximum temperatures for LIB 1, 2, 3, and 4 are 401.9 ℃, 437.0 ℃, 510.3 ℃ and 524.7 ℃, respectively, exhibiting an increasing tendency, which can be attributed to the pre-heating effect.

claims.claim: During TR propagation, the energy released by the TR battery was responsible for triggering TR in its adjacent battery, accounting for more than 65%.
claims.quote: During TR propagation, the energy released by the TR battery was responsible for the triggering of TR in its adjacent battery, accounting for more than 65%.

claims.claim: In module 2, inserting a 3-mm aerogel felt (175 × 200 mm) between adjacent batteries prevented TR propagation so that only the initial battery suffered TR.
claims.quote: As shown in Fig. 6(a), only the initial battery suffered from TR due to the presence of aerogel felts, demonstrating that TR propagation was prevented by the aerogel felts.

claims.claim: In module 2, after TR of LIB 1, LIB 2 safety venting occurred at 5023 s and T2-f and T2-b peaked at 204.4 ℃ and 147.4 ℃, respectively.
claims.quote: With the increase of temperature in LIB 2, the incident of SV was triggered at 5023 s, and T2-f and T2-b peaked at 204.4 ℃ and 147.4 ℃, respectively.

claims.claim: For module 1, the net heat transferred to LIB 2, 3 and 4 before their TR were 308.4 kJ, 310.8 kJ and 354.2 kJ, respectively.
claims.quote: For the module without prevention, the net heat transferred to LIB 2, 3 and 4 before their TR are 308.4 kJ, 310.8 kJ and 354.2 kJ, respectively

claims.claim: Abnormal heat is generated when the battery temperature exceeds 60 °C.
claims.quote:
Abnormal heat is generated when the battery temperature exceeds
◦
60 
C [34].

claims.claim: A temperature threshold of 60 °C is set for switching models; below 60 °C the LSTM model predicts temperature, and above 60 °C thermal images are captured and MMTPFNN predicts TR propagation.
claims.quote:
A switching strategy of the prediction model is established to improve the
applicability of the model with the temperature threshold of 60 
C. When the battery is in a safe temperature
◦
range (below 60 
C), the long short-term memory (LSTM) model is run to predict the battery temperature. Once
◦
the battery temperature is detected above 60 
C, the thermal image is captured, and the MMTPFNN model is run
to predict the TR propagation.

claims.claim: The heating power used in the experiments is computed by Pheat = (−15.41Ecell + 4.72mcell).
claims.quote: Pheat = (cid:0) 15.41Ecell + 4.72mcell

claims.claim: The heating power calculated by the given equation is 60 W.
claims.quote:
The heating power calculated by equation (1)
is 60 W.

claims.claim: In the simulation model settings, the average temperature at which the voltage drop occurs is 120 °C.
claims.quote:
◦
average temperature at which the voltage drop occurs is 120 
C.

claims.claim: Commercial 21700 NMC cylindrical LIBs (21 mm diameter, 70 mm height) were used.
claims.quote: Commercial 21700 LIBs of lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) cathode (Tesla Model 3 EVs, 21 mm in diameter and 70 mm in height) were used in this work

claims.claim: All tests were conducted at 100% SOC, with environmental pressures of 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa (each repeated twice).
claims.quote: in this work, 101, 90, 70, 50, and 30 kPa were considered in Table 1... To reduce experimental error, each set of experiments was repeated twice... Subsequently, the batteries were charged to 100% SOC

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature Ttr showed an upward trend as environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa.
claims.quote: The results indicated that Ttr followed an upward trend as the environmental pressure decreased from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: The time interval from safety venting to thermal runaway tinterval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa.
claims.quote: The tinterval reduced from 48 s to 22 s when the pressure declined from 101 to 30 kPa.

claims.claim: For the given test conditions, flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the maximum jet flame height occurred at 50 kPa with a height of 0.28 m.
claims.quote: for given test conditions, the phenomenon of flame lift-off was not observed at different pressures, and the jet flame height reached the maximum at 50 kPa, which was 0.28 m.

claims.claim: The battery cell is modeled as a constant heat source at 500 K, and the outer wall of PCM2 is set to an ambient temperature of 300 K.
claims.quote: The battery cell, which acts as a heat source with a temperature of 500 K, ... The outer wall of PCM2 is specified as a cold source with an ambient temperature of 300 K.

claims.claim: PCM1 is RT82 (closest to the battery) and PCM2 is RT27 (outermost layer) in the trans-radial arrangement.
claims.quote: The PCM closest to the battery cell is referred to as RT82, and the PCM in the outermost layer is known as RT27.

claims.claim: The diameters of PCM1 and PCM2 are 30 mm and 42 mm, respectively.
claims.quote: The diameters of PCM1 and PCM2 are 30 mm and 42 mm, respectively.

claims.claim: The study varies nanoparticle volume fraction from 2% to 6% using Al2O3 and SWCNT dispersed within the PCM.
claims.quote: In this study, two types of nanoparticles, Al2O3 and SWCNT, are dispersed within the PCM to assess their thermal performance. The effects of nanoparticles are evaluated by varying the volume fraction from 2 % to 6 %.

claims.claim: For RT27 (PCM2), SWCNT nanoparticles increase the melting rate by up to 66.54% at 6% volume fraction compared to the pure PCM of RT27.
claims.quote: at 6 %, the rate is enhanced by up to 66.54 % compared to the pure PCM of RT27.

claims.claim: With constant heating power of 265 W, thermal runaway was triggered after 225 s of heating using the heating coil.
claims.quote: Specifically, thermal runaway is triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil

claims.claim: Using the heating coil, thermal runaway was triggered at 140.07 °C, which is 48.94 °C higher than with the heating rod and 38.06 °C higher than with the heating plate.
claims.quote:
reaching 140.07
C, which is 48.94
C and 38.06
C higher than the temperatures triggered by the rod and plate, respectively.

claims.claim: Triggering times were reduced by 34.00 s and 57.79 s (compared to the heating rod and heating plate, respectively) when using the heating coil.
claims.quote: Triggering times are reduced by 34.00 s and 57.79 s, respectively.

claims.claim: Peak battery temperatures after thermal runaway were 326.03 °C for heating coil, 460.35 °C for heating plate, and 508.67 °C for heating rod.
claims.quote:
the following order from lowest to highest: heating coil (326.03
C),
heating plate (460.35
C), and heating rod (508.67
C)

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak pressure was 430.43 kPa.
claims.quote: the corresponding peak pressure is the highest at 430.43 kPa.
